interpretation: M1 (constant lag) substantially and robustly outperforms M0 (flow-only) across every criterion (level RMSE, LOOCV RMSE, AIC, BIC): M1 beats M0 by AIC in 64% of countries, by BIC in 64%, and by LOOCV in 69%. This confirms that a spending-to-outcome lag exists, supporting a stock-based view of health-capital investment. The time-varying extension M2 does not improve on M1: M2 is favoured over M1 by AIC in only 0% of countries and by BIC in 0%, and the median level-RMSE difference is negligible (0.208 vs 0.207). The drift parameter mu_H1 is not robustly identified (bimodal, boundary-dominated across countries). We therefore find no reliable evidence for a time-varying lag; the robust finding is the existence of a constant lag (M1 vs M0), not its time-variation (M2 vs M1).
